title: End of module: Estimating the Cyclic Spectrum in Practice

questions.text: A raw 'cyclic periodogram' computed from one finite recording:
choices: Is just as inconsistent an estimator of the true SCF as the raw periodogram was for the ordinary PSD
Is automatically a consistent estimator, unlike the ordinary periodogram
Cannot be computed at all without infinite data
questions.explanation: The same variance problem that afflicts the raw periodogram afflicts a raw cyclic periodogram — finite data gives an inconsistent estimate whose variance doesn't shrink with recording length alone.

questions.text: Time-smoothing cyclic spectral estimators are the direct analogue of which ordinary spectral-estimation technique?
choices: Welch's/Bartlett's method — averaging periodograms across shifted or overlapping segments
Zero-padding the FFT before computing a spectrum
Increasing the sample rate of the recording
questions.explanation: Time-smoothing generalizes Welch/Bartlett segment-averaging into the two-dimensional (f, α) plane, applying the same variance-reduction idea over an extra dimension.

questions.text: FAM (FFT Accumulation Method) and SSCA (Strip Spectral Correlation Algorithm) both:
choices: Are FFT-based algorithms that efficiently estimate the SCF over frequency and cycle frequency, organized differently for computational efficiency
Compute unrelated quantities to each other
Require noise-free input data to function
questions.explanation: FAM and SSCA both compute the same underlying quantity — a smoothed SCF estimate — using different organizations of FFT-based computation, each suited to different implementation tradeoffs.

questions.text: Compared to estimating an ordinary PSD, estimating the SCF over N frequency bins and M candidate cycle frequencies is:
choices: Noticeably more computationally expensive, since the search now spans both dimensions
Exactly the same cost, since α comes for free once you have an FFT
Cheaper, because fewer frequency bins are needed
questions.explanation: Searching over both f and α means the computation scales with both dimensions rather than one, making real-time cyclic spectral estimation genuinely more demanding than an ordinary PSD.

questions.text: Why is efficient hardware (e.g. FPGA-accelerated) implementation of FAM/SSCA considered a legitimately open engineering area rather than a solved problem?
choices: Real-time cyclic spectral estimation is computationally heavy, and efficient hardware mappings of these algorithms are still actively developed
There are no real-time applications that need cyclic spectral estimation
questions.explanation: The two-dimensional search over f and α makes cyclic spectral estimation computationally demanding, and mapping FAM/SSCA efficiently onto real hardware for real-time use (spectrum sensing, modulation classification) remains an active area, not a finished textbook exercise.

questions.text: One acronym: the algorithm that organizes cyclic spectral estimation around narrow frequency 'strips' is called the ___.
questions.explanation: SSCA (Strip Spectral Correlation Algorithm) organizes its computation around narrow frequency strips, distinct from FAM's time-block-based organization.

questions.text: One acronym: the algorithm that builds up the (f, α) surface from banks of short-time FFTs across overlapping time blocks is called the ___.
questions.explanation: FAM (FFT Accumulation Method) computes overlapping short-time FFTs across time blocks and combines them to estimate the spectral correlation function.

questions.text: If searching one candidate cycle frequency costs roughly the same as one ordinary PSD estimate, and your case needs to search M = 40 candidate cycle frequencies, roughly how many times more expensive is a naive (non-FAM/SSCA) cyclic spectral search than a single ordinary PSD estimate?
questions.explanation: A naive search repeats roughly one PSD-estimate's worth of work per candidate cycle frequency, so 40 candidates costs roughly 40x an ordinary single-frequency-axis PSD estimate — precisely the cost that FAM/SSCA's smarter computational organization is designed to reduce.

questions.text: Cumulative review: the reason estimating the cyclic spectrum needs variance-reduction techniques at all traces back to which earlier result?
choices: The raw periodogram (and by extension the raw cyclic periodogram) is an inconsistent estimator whose variance doesn't shrink with recording length alone
The Wiener–Khinchin theorem only applies to noise-free signals
Eigenvalues of a covariance matrix are always negative for real signals
questions.explanation: From spectral-estimation: the periodogram's core pathology (inconsistency) is exactly what motivates Bartlett/Welch averaging there, and the same pathology — now in two dimensions — motivates time-smoothing and frequency-smoothing for the SCF here.
